content.full_text:
CITY COUNCIL

THOMAS A. PETERSON
City Manager

JAMES W. PINKERTON, Mayor
PHILLIP A. PENNINO
Mayor Pro Tempore
DAVID M. HINCHMAN
JACK A. SIEGLOCK
JOHN R. (Randy) SNIDER

CITY OF LODI

ALICE M. REIMCHE
City Clerk

CITY HALL, 221 WEST PINE STREET
P.O. BOX 3006
LODI, CALIFORNIA 95241-1910
(209) 334-5634
FAX (209) 333-6795

BOB McNATT
City Attorney

June 4, 1992

Deanne Stone, Esquire
Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Subject: INFORMAL ADVICE LETTER, YOUR FILE NO. I-90-714

Dear Ms. Stone:

In February 1991, you authored the above informal advice letter regarding Councilmember Phillip Pennino. One of the issues addressed involved potential conflicts where the City was annexing property previously served by Pacific Gas & Electric Company (PG&E) which is Mr. Pennino's employer. The potential conflict comes from the fact that the City of Lodi also has a municipally-owned electric utility. The questions involved calculation of hypothetical losses of customers by PG&E when the City annexes land and begins providing its own electric utility service. Recent events have put a new slant on this question.

A few weeks ago, the City became aware that PG&E was, for the first time, proposing to provide electric utility service for these new homes to be built inside the City on the newly-annexed land. In the past, when land was annexed and developed, the City routinely assumed the duty to provide electrical service, displacing PG&E. It now appears the City and PG&E may be in competition to serve new development.

In the past, annexed areas were almost exclusively bare land or possibly farm land with a single home. The value of the accounts lost by PG&E was thus always far below the threshold amounts found in Regulation 18702.2(a).

It now appears that the question [for purposes of Regulation 18702.2(a)] is not what the value is of PG&E's lost customers when land is annexed, but rather the value of the potential customers PG&E might get if land is developed and it is successful in its proposal to provide electrical service. Any vote for annexation of residential property now carries with it the possibility that PG&E will want to serve the area annexed. It is presumed that Councilmember Pennino would be required to evaluate the possibility of a conflict at that point. This leads to the two critical questions which have now arisen:


[illegible]

PPPC
June 4, 1992
Page Two


1)  In determining threshold amounts under Regulation 18702.2(a), should
    the potential impact on PG&E of each separate annexation be valued
    individually or must all annexations where PG&E has the potential to
    provide electric service be cumulated for calculating financial
    impacts?

2)  If annexations are to be cumulated for purposes of this regulation,
    then what period of time should be used (for example, should all
    annexations during a one-year or five-year period be evaluated)?.

It would appear that if each annexation is taken individually, threshold
amounts found in Regulation 18702.2(a) would not be reached very often. It
is my understanding that it costs PG&E approximately $1500 to install
facilities for serving each home in new development areas, and that each
home's annual bill for electric utility service averages between $900 and
$1000.

Bearing in mind that Lodi has a growth control ordinance which limits new
residential development to approximately 400 units per year, this would
mean that it would cost PG&E approximately $600,000 to install facilities
for all new homes and would generate an annual income to PG&E of
approximately $360,000.  These numbers do not appear to exceed threshold
figures for Fortune 500 companies, as specified in Section 18702.2(a).

As a sidelight, Councilmember Pennino did vote on several recent
annexations which PG&E now proposes to serve. However, these annexation
votes occurred before the Company announced its intentions and
Councilmember Pennino had no prior knowledge of the Company's desire to
serve the areas.

Your assistance and advice is requested.

Sincerely yours,


BOB W. McNATT
City Attorney


COI13.4/TXTA.01V
